data_basis.description: 跨 Product Family 共性启发式规则，基于 5 份 CER 范本横向比较 + 64 条 NB 审评缺陷模式提炼

heuristics.description: Universal decision framework for whether and how to conduct equivalence demonstration

applicability.exception_reason: Innovative Class III devices with no comparable products should explicitly claim non-equivalence

rules.rule: Step 1 — Market Scan: List ALL marketed similar devices (minimum 2-3 candidates)
rules.rationale: NB frequently challenges equivalence breadth when only one comparator is cited

rules.rule: Step 2 — Data Intent: Confirm plan to reference equivalent device's clinical data; if NO, simplify to background comparison only
rules.rationale: Unnecessary equivalence论证 creates compliance burden without clinical value

rules.rule: Step 3 — Three-Pillar Viability: Verify MDR Article 61 technical/biological/clinical comparability is achievable; if NO, select alternative candidate
rules.rationale: MDCG 2020-5 mandates three-pillar structure; incomplete pillars = direct NB finding

rules.rule: Expected purpose MUST be identical between subject and equivalent device — this is the non-negotiable core of equivalence
rules.rationale: Any deviation in intended purpose invalidates the entire equivalence claim

rules.rule: Each difference MUST have an Impact Assessment: 'This difference does not affect clinical safety and performance because [reasoning with standard/test data reference]'
rules.rationale: NB DO-001 findings frequently stem from unexplained design differences

templates.content: Equivalence shall be demonstrated according to the Regulation (EU) 2017/745. The requirements from MEDDEV 2.7/1 Rev. 4 and MDCG 2020-5 would also be taken into consideration. MDR requires that technical, biological and clinical characteristics are considered when demonstrating equivalence to another device.
templates.source: Common pattern across 帕姆/鱼跃/海杰亚

templates.content: Equivalence is not claimed for the device under evaluation. The device represents a novel technology for which no equivalent device has been identified on the market. Therefore, the clinical evaluation relies solely on [clinical investigation data / literature data].

The [evaluated device / equivalent device] uses [material A / material B]. Although there is a difference in [aspect], this does not affect the clinical safety and performance because [reasoning, e.g., both materials have been demonstrated to be biocompatible per ISO 10993-1 for the intended contact type and duration].

checklist: [ ] At least 2-3 potential equivalent devices identified through market scan
[ ] Equivalent device has CE mark or equivalent regulatory approval in target market
[ ] Plan to reference equivalent device's clinical data is confirmed
[ ] Three-pillar comparison table (technical/biological/clinical) is complete
[ ] Each difference has explicit Impact Assessment with reasoning
[ ] Expected purpose is identical (verbal confirmation)
[ ] §4.4.2 contains dedicated literature search subsection for equivalent device
[ ] Equivalent device literature listed separately in Annex

common_pitfalls.pitfall: Equivalent device selection too narrow (only 1 candidate)
common_pitfalls.fix: Expand to 2-3 candidates, select closest for detailed论证
Every difference must have 'does not affect safety/performance' conclusion

common_pitfalls.pitfall: Equivalence claimed but no separate literature search for equivalent device
common_pitfalls.fix: §4.4.2 must have dedicated subsection

common_pitfalls.pitfall: Using unpublished proprietary data of equivalent device
common_pitfalls.fix: Only use published literature data from SOTA search

heuristics.injection_point: Writer Agent §4.2 and §4.4.2 — before generating equivalence section
heuristics.writer_guard_snippet: When writing the Equivalence Demonstration section: (1) ALWAYS open with regulatory basis statement, (2) Three-pillar table is MANDATORY if equivalence is claimed, (3) Every cell in 'Comparison' column must be Same/Similar/Different with justification, (4) Expected purpose row is NON-NEGOTIABLE — any difference here invalidates the entire claim.

heuristics.description: Universal GSPR analysis structure that satisfies NB review expectations across all device categories

rules.rule: Every GSPR item must have ONE of: (a) Full compliance evidence, OR (b) Justified non-applicability with detailed rationale
rules.rationale: BSI flags GS-001 in 18% of findings; 'N/A' without justification is a direct Major finding

rules.rule: GSPR analysis MUST follow five-segment structure: (1) Standard/Guideline reference, (2) Test/verification method, (3) Evidence summary, (4) Gap identification, (5) Risk control measure
rules.rationale: Structured GSPR analysis demonstrates systematic compliance thinking; unstructured lists invite NB scrutiny

rules.rule: Non-applicable GSPR items require: (a) Explicit statement of non-applicability, (b) Technical justification tied to device design, (c) Reference to risk management file
rules.rationale: 苏州晶乐 5/5 findings = GS-001 from inadequate non-applicability rationale; this is a systemic trap
rules.source: NB defect GS-001 persistent pattern (苏州晶乐 R1→R3)

rules.rule: GSPR checklist numbering must align with Annex I of MDR 2017/745; do NOT use internal company numbering without mapping
rules.rationale: NB reviewers reference MDR Annex I directly; non-standard numbering creates friction

templates.content:
**GSPR [X.X] — [Title]**

1. **Regulatory Reference**: MDR 2017/745 Annex I, Chapter [X], GSPR [X.X]; [ISO/IEC standard reference]

2. **Verification Method**: [Test method / design review / risk analysis / clinical evaluation]

3. **Evidence Summary**: [Summary of test results / design documentation / clinical data supporting compliance]

4. **Gap Assessment**: [None / Minor gap identified: [description] / See risk control measure below]

5. **Risk Control**: [If gap exists: mitigation measure with verification plan]
templates.usage: §4.7 GSPR逐条分析 or standalone GSPR annex

templates.content:
**GSPR [X.X] — [Title] — NOT APPLICABLE**

Justification: This GSPR applies to [device category description]. The [device name] is [device type] and does not [specific characteristic that makes GSPR irrelevant]. This is documented in the Risk Management File (Ref: [doc ref]) under Hazard [H-XXX] analysis, which confirms that [specific risk] is controlled by [alternative measure] and does not require compliance with GSPR [X.X].

checklist: [ ] Every GSPR item has compliance status (Applicable / Not Applicable / Partially Applicable)
[ ] Every 'Not Applicable' has detailed technical justification
[ ] Justification references device design characteristics (not generic statements)
[ ] Risk management file cross-reference exists for non-applicable items
[ ] Five-segment structure followed for applicable items
[ ] GSPR numbering aligns with MDR Annex I (not internal numbering)
[ ] Evidence references are traceable to Annex or technical documentation

common_pitfalls.fix: Always provide technical rationale tied to device design

common_pitfalls.pitfall: Generic justification ('not relevant to this device type')
common_pitfalls.fix: Reference specific device characteristics and risk management analysis
Use MDR Annex I full chapter list as master checklist

common_pitfalls.pitfall: Evidence references point to non-existent documents
common_pitfalls.fix: Pre-verify all referenced document IDs exist in technical file

heuristics.injection_point: Writer Agent §4.7 or standalone GSPR annex — before generating GSPR analysis
heuristics.writer_guard_snippet: When writing GSPR analysis: (1) NEVER use 'N/A' without 2+ sentence technical justification, (2) Every justification must reference a SPECIFIC device characteristic, (3) Five-segment structure is MANDATORY for applicable items, (4) Non-applicable items require risk management file cross-reference. BSI will scrutinize EVERY non-applicability rationale.

heuristics.description: Universal conclusion structure ensuring NB reviewers can verify every claim is evidence-backed

rules.rule: Conclusion MUST contain six elements: (1) Evaluation scope restatement, (2) Evidence summary (quantity + quality), (3) GSPR compliance statement, (4) Benefit-risk conclusion, (5) PMCF/PMS commitment, (6) Overall acceptability verdict
rules.rationale: Incomplete conclusions are the #1 reason NB requests CER revision; missing any element creates a gap NB will exploit

rules.rule: Every conclusion claim MUST be traceable to a specific section in §4 with table/figure reference
rules.rationale: §4→§5 is a rigid dependency chain; conclusions without evidence anchors are 'bald statements' that NB rejects

rules.rule: Benefit-risk conclusion must be quantitative where possible: cite specific endpoint values, patient numbers, confidence intervals
rules.rationale: Qualitative-only benefit-risk statements ('acceptable safety profile') are flagged as insufficient evidence

rules.rule: PMCF/PMS commitment must specify: next evaluation date, planned studies (if any), data sources, trigger conditions for unscheduled re-evaluation
rules.rationale: PM-001 findings frequently stem from vague PMCF commitments; specificity is the defense

templates.content:
**1. Scope Restatement**: This Clinical Evaluation Report covers the [device name] ([model list]) for the intended purpose of [intended purpose], as described in §2.

**2. Evidence Summary**: The clinical evaluation is based on [N] clinical studies ([total patients] patients), [M] literature sources, [P] years of post-market surveillance data, and [Q] vigilance database searches. The overall evidence quality is assessed as [high/moderate/low] per the appraisal in §4.5.

**3. GSPR Compliance**: The device complies with the General Safety and Performance Requirements of MDR Annex I, as demonstrated in §4.7/[GSPR Annex Ref]. All applicable GSPRs are addressed with appropriate evidence.

**4. Benefit-Risk Assessment**: Based on the analysis in §4.7.2, the clinical benefits of [specific benefits] outweigh the identified risks of [specific risks], with an acceptable benefit-risk ratio supported by [quantitative data reference: Table X].

**5. PMCF/PMS Commitment**: Post-market clinical follow-up will be conducted through [data sources]. The next scheduled clinical evaluation will be performed on [date] or earlier if [trigger conditions]. [Planned PMCF study details if applicable].

**6. Overall Verdict**: Based on the available clinical evidence, the [device name] is considered [state: acceptable for CE marking / requiring further evidence] for the intended purpose stated in §2.

checklist: [ ] All six elements present in §5
[ ] Every claim has §4 cross-reference with table/figure number
[ ] Benefit-risk statement includes quantitative data
[ ] PMCF commitment specifies date, data sources, triggers
[ ] Overall verdict uses standardized wording ('acceptable for CE marking')
[ ] No new claims introduced in §5 that are not supported in §4
[ ] Conclusion length proportionate to CER total length (~3-5% of total)

common_pitfalls.pitfall: Introducing new claims in conclusion not supported in §4
common_pitfalls.consequence: NB requests evidence addition or claim removal
common_pitfalls.fix: Cross-check every conclusion sentence against §4
Include specific endpoint values and patient numbers
Specify date, data sources, trigger conditions
Final cross-chapter validation (CCV-04)

heuristics.injection_point: Writer Agent §5 — after §4 is complete, before generating conclusions
heuristics.writer_guard_snippet: When writing §5 Conclusions: (1) This section ONLY summarizes what has ALREADY been proven in §4 — NO new claims allowed, (2) Every sentence must have a §4 cross-reference, (3) Benefit-risk MUST include numbers (not just 'acceptable'), (4) PMCF commitment needs specific date and triggers. The conclusion is NOT a place for persuasion — it is a compliance checkpoint.

heuristics.description: Universal framework for structuring SOTA section that satisfies both clinical rigor and NB review expectations

rules.rule: SOTA section MUST answer three core questions: (Q1) What is the current standard of care? (Q2) What are the safety/performance benchmarks? (Q3) What gaps does this device address?
rules.rationale: SOTA is not a literature review — it is a strategic positioning of the device within clinical practice; missing any question leaves NB without context

rules.rule: Search strategy must follow PICO framework and be documented in a structured table with: databases, search date, terms, filters, hits, relevant hits
rules.rationale: Undocumented search strategies are non-reproducible and invite NB challenge on SOTA adequacy

rules.rule: Safety/Performance Endpoints table must include: endpoint name, definition, measurement method, SOTA benchmark value, source reference
rules.rationale: §4.7 analysis depends on §3 benchmark values; missing benchmarks make analysis unverifiable

rules.rule: Alternative treatment options must cover: pharmacological, surgical, interventional, and device-based alternatives where applicable
rules.rationale: Incomplete alternative treatment coverage signals insufficient clinical context understanding

templates.content:
| Database | Search Date | Search Terms | Language | Date Range | Hits | Relevant Hits |
|----------|-------------|--------------|----------|------------|------|---------------|
| PubMed | YYYY-MM-DD | [Boolean search string] | English | [From-To] | [N] | [M] |
| Embase | YYYY-MM-DD | [Boolean search string] | English | [From-To] | [N] | [M] |

| Endpoint | Definition | Measurement | SOTA Benchmark | Source |
|----------|------------|-------------|----------------|--------|
| [Name] | [Clinical definition] | [Method] | [Value ± CI] | [Ref] |

checklist: [ ] Q1 answered: Current standard of care described with references
[ ] Q2 answered: Safety/performance benchmarks quantified with sources
[ ] Q3 answered: Device positioning gap clearly stated
[ ] PICO-based search strategy documented in structured table
[ ] At least 2 databases searched (PubMed + Embase minimum)
[ ] Search date recorded to day-level precision
[ ] PRISMA-style flow diagram or equivalent included
[ ] Alternative treatments cover all major therapeutic categories
[ ] Clinical practice guidelines from ≥2 regions cited

common_pitfalls.pitfall: SOTA reads like a general disease review without device positioning
common_pitfalls.fix: Frame every SOTA element around the three core questions
Include structured search protocol table
Endpoint table with SOTA benchmark column
Minimum PubMed + Embase; Class III should add Cochrane

heuristics.injection_point: Writer Agent §3 — before generating SOTA section
heuristics.writer_guard_snippet: When writing §3 SOTA: (1) This is NOT a literature review — it is CLINICAL CONTEXT for the device, (2) Three questions MUST be answered: What is standard care? What are benchmarks? What gap does this device fill? (3) Search strategy table is MANDATORY — undocumented search = non-reproducible = NB challenge, (4) Every benchmark value needs a source. SOTA sets the foundation for everything in §4 — get it wrong and the entire CER collapses.

heuristics.description: Cross-domain universal checklist ensuring device description satisfies all NB bodies' #1 concern (DO-001 at 40.6%)

rules.rule: Device identity anchor MUST include: name, model/variant list, EMDN code, MDR Class+Rule, intended purpose
rules.rationale: This is the #1 defect across ALL NB bodies (DO-001: 40.6%); missing any element triggers immediate Major finding

rules.rule: For EVERY product variant: document (a) clinical/engineering reason for existence, (b) impact on safety/performance/usability, (c) clinical scenario for use
rules.rationale: TÜV SÜD pursues variant rationale across R1→R3; BSI Technical Review challenges unexplained design differences
rules.source: TÜV SÜD persistent DO-001 pattern (青岛德迈迪 R1→R3)

rules.rule: Intended purpose must specify: patient population (age/gender/condition), clinical setting (hospital/ambulatory/home), intended users (physician/nurse/patient), AND contraindications
rules.rationale: Vague intended purpose ('for treatment of X') is the #2 trigger for DO-001; specificity is the defense
rules.source: NB defect DO-001 analysis across all projects

rules.rule: Novel features MUST be explicitly described with: what is novel, why it matters clinically, what evidence supports the novelty claim
rules.rationale: 心擎 7/8 findings focused on device description because novel VAD technology required exhaustive documentation

rules.rule: Marketing history must include: first approval date, markets authorized, sales volume (if available), vigilance summary (serious incidents, recalls)
rules.rationale: Marketing history is 5/5 consistent across reports but frequently under-detailed; NB checks for completeness

templates.content:
| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Device Name | [通用名] |
| Model/Variant | [型号列表] |
| EMDN Code | [EMDN代码] |
| MDR Classification | Class [I/IIa/IIb/III], Rule [X] |
| Intended Purpose | [一句话描述] |
| Patient Population | [年龄/性别/疾病状态] |
| Clinical Setting | [医院/门诊/家庭] |
| Intended Users | [医师/护士/患者] |

| Variant | Design Rationale | Safety Impact | Performance Impact | Clinical Scenario |
|---------|-----------------|---------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| [Variant A] | [为什么存在此变型] | [对安全性的影响] | [对性能的影响] | [使用场景] |
| [Variant B] | [为什么存在此变型] | [对安全性的影响] | [对性能的影响] | [使用场景] |

checklist: [ ] Device name is consistent across ALL CER sections
[ ] All models/variants listed with unique identifiers
[ ] EMDN code verified against latest EMDN nomenclature
[ ] MDR Class and Rule correctly assigned
[ ] Intended purpose includes patient population, setting, users
[ ] Contraindications listed (not just 'see IFU')
[ ] Every variant has design rationale
[ ] Novel features explicitly described with evidence
[ ] Previous generations / similar devices referenced
[ ] Marketing history includes approvals, markets, vigilance summary
[ ] Accessories and compatible devices listed
[ ] Principle of operation described in clinician-understandable terms

common_pitfalls.pitfall: Device name inconsistent across CER sections
common_pitfalls.fix: Use find/replace to verify name consistency before submission

common_pitfalls.consequence: DO-001 Major finding (TÜV SÜD will persist R1→R3)
common_pitfalls.fix: Every variant row needs design rationale column
Include population, setting, users, contraindications

common_pitfalls.pitfall: Marketing history only lists countries without dates
common_pitfalls.fix: Include first approval date for each market

heuristics.injection_point: Writer Agent §2 — before generating device description; AND as Gate check before any downstream section
heuristics.writer_guard_snippet: When writing §2 Device Description: (1) This is the MOST SCRUTINIZED section — DO-001 is 40.6% of all NB findings, (2) Device identity anchor (name/model/EMDN/Class/Purpose) is NON-NEGOTIABLE, (3) EVERY variant needs design rationale — 'obvious' or 'implied' is NOT acceptable to NB, (4) Intended purpose must be specific enough that a regulator can verify it against clinical evidence in §4. TÜV SÜD will ask the same question THREE TIMES if the rationale is incomplete.

heuristics.description: Cross-domain rules for clinical evidence sufficiency based on MDR Article 61 and NB review patterns

rules.rule: Evidence hierarchy per MDR Article 61: (1) Clinical data from equivalent device → (2) Clinical investigations → (3) Published literature → (4) Post-market surveillance. Lower tiers must justify why higher tiers are insufficient.
rules.rationale: Evidence hierarchy inversion (e.g., relying on literature when CI data exists) is a direct NB challenge point

rules.rule: Literature-based evaluation must include: systematic search protocol, inclusion/exclusion criteria, PRISMA-style flow diagram, quality appraisal of included studies, quantitative synthesis where possible
rules.rationale: Unsystematic literature reviews are rejected as insufficient evidence; PRISMA compliance is the minimum bar

rules.rule: Vigilance data must cover: MHRA, BfArM, FDA MAUDE (minimum); Swissmedic and TGA for Class III; search date must be within 6 months of CER date
rules.rationale: Incomplete vigilance coverage signals inadequate safety monitoring; stale data (>6 months) invites challenge on currency

rules.rule: Clinical investigation data must include: study design, patient population, endpoints, statistical analysis plan, results with confidence intervals, limitations, and conclusion
rules.rationale: Incomplete CI reporting is flagged as CL-001; 鑫君特 Clinical Oversight found sample size and follow-up deficiencies

rules.rule: Safety data tables must stratify by: severity (serious/non-serious), relationship (related/unrelated/unknown), and outcome (resolved/ongoing/fatal)
rules.rationale: Unstratified safety data prevents meaningful benefit-risk analysis; this is a recurring NB challenge
rules.source: CER_05_TypeA (safety data tables) + NB defect BR-001

templates.content:
Records identified from databases (n=[N]):
  PubMed (n=[N1])
  Embase (n=[N2])
  Cochrane (n=[N3])
  ↓
Records after duplicate removal (n=[N4])
  ↓
Records screened by title/abstract (n=[N5])
  Excluded (n=[N6])
  ↓
Full-text articles assessed (n=[N7])
  Excluded with reasons (n=[N8])
  ↓
Studies included in qualitative synthesis (n=[N9])
  ↓
Studies included in quantitative synthesis (n=[N10])

checklist: [ ] Evidence hierarchy followed (higher tiers prioritized)
[ ] Literature search protocol documented and reproducible
[ ] PRISMA flow diagram included
[ ] Quality appraisal method stated (e.g., Newcastle-Ottawa, GRADE)
[ ] MHRA + BfArM + FDA MAUDE searched (minimum)
[ ] Vigilance search date within 6 months of CER date
[ ] CI data includes: design, population, endpoints, SAP, results, limitations
[ ] Safety data stratified by severity/relationship/outcome
[ ] All data sources traceable to Annex or external document references

common_pitfalls.pitfall: Evidence hierarchy inverted (literature preferred over available CI data)
common_pitfalls.fix: Use highest available tier with justification for lower tier use
Include PRISMA flow in §4.4 or Annex
Update vigilance search within 6 months of submission
Use three-dimensional stratification table

heuristics.injection_point: Writer Agent §4.3-4.7 — before generating clinical evidence sections
heuristics.writer_guard_snippet: When writing clinical evidence sections: (1) Evidence hierarchy is LAW — MDR Article 61 defines the order; justify ANY deviation, (2) Literature review without PRISMA = unsystematic = rejected, (3) Vigilance data must be FRESH (<6 months) and COMPREHENSIVE (MHRA+BfArM+FDA minimum), (4) Safety data MUST be stratified — raw event lists are insufficient for benefit-risk analysis.

heuristics.description: Cross-domain writing style rules ensuring CER readability and regulatory professionalism

rules.rule: Sentence length: §2 device description ≤35 words; §4 evidence analysis 25-30 words; §5 conclusions ≤20 words
rules.rationale: Long sentences in conclusions reduce readability and clarity; NB reviewers prefer direct statements

rules.rule: Paragraph length: 25-40 words per paragraph; table-adjacent paragraphs 10-20 words
rules.rationale: Overly long paragraphs reduce scannability; regulatory reviewers scan for key information

rules.rule: Passive voice: 15-25% of total sentences; higher in §2 device description, lower in §4 analysis
rules.rationale: Excessive passive voice obscures agency; insufficient passive voice sounds too assertive for evidence sections

rules.rule: Tense consistency: §2 device description uses present tense; §4 evidence analysis uses past tense for completed studies, present tense for ongoing/general statements; §5 conclusions uses present tense
rules.rationale: Tense inconsistency signals poor editorial control and undermines credibility

rules.rule: PEEL paragraph structure: Point (1 sentence) → Evidence (1-2 sentences) → Explanation (1-2 sentences) → Link (1 sentence)
rules.rationale: PEEL structure ensures every paragraph makes a claim, supports it with evidence, explains relevance, and connects to next paragraph

templates.content:
**Point**: [Main claim of this paragraph]
**Evidence**: [Data/study/reference supporting the claim]
**Explanation**: [Why this evidence matters for the device evaluation]
**Link**: [How this connects to the next paragraph or section]

checklist: [ ] §2 sentences ≤35 words
[ ] §4 sentences 25-30 words
[ ] §5 sentences ≤20 words
[ ] Paragraphs 25-40 words (table-adjacent 10-20)
[ ] Passive voice 15-25% overall
[ ] Tense consistent within each section
[ ] PEEL structure followed for analytical paragraphs
[ ] No bullet-point-only sections (must have connecting prose)
[ ] Hedging language balanced (not too certain, not too vague)

common_pitfalls.fix: Break into shorter declarative sentences
Section-level tense audit pass

common_pitfalls.consequence: Reads as draft, not finalized document
common_pitfalls.fix: Every list must be introduced and summarized in prose

heuristics.injection_point: Writer Agent — style layer applied to all output; Gate TV-09 (Style Compliance Check)
heuristics.writer_guard_snippet: Writing style rules: (1) Sentence length varies by section: §2≤35w, §4=25-30w, §5≤20w, (2) Paragraphs 25-40w with PEEL structure, (3) Passive voice 15-25%, (4) Tense: §2=present, §4=past for studies/present for general, §5=present, (5) EVERY list needs prose introduction and summary. This is not creative writing — it is regulatory documentation. Clarity > Elegance.

heuristics.description: Universal rules ensuring data consistency across CER chapters — prevents DT-001 data inconsistency findings

rules.rule: §2 device specifications MUST match §4.2 equivalence table specifications; any discrepancy must be explained with version control reference
rules.rationale: §2→§4.2 is a rigid dependency chain; specification mismatch is a direct DT-001 finding

rules.rule: §3 SOTA benchmark values MUST be the same values used in §4.7 analysis; benchmark updates require explicit changelog entry
rules.rationale: SOTA benchmarks are the analytical foundation; changing them without documentation collapses analysis validity

rules.rule: §4.4 literature count MUST equal Annex literature list count; if different, explain exclusions
rules.rationale: Literature count mismatch is the most common DT-001 trigger; NB reviewers cross-check these numbers

rules.rule: §5 conclusion claims MUST have exact §4 cross-references; no conclusion claim without a corresponding §4 analysis paragraph
rules.rationale: §4→§5 is rigid dependency; orphan conclusions are 'bald statements' NB rejects

rules.rule: Device name MUST be identical across ALL chapters (including Annexes); use find/replace verification before submission
rules.rationale: Name inconsistency is the simplest DT-001 trigger and the easiest to prevent

templates.content:
| Data Element | §2 Value | §4 Value | §5 Value | Annex Value | Status |
|-------------|----------|----------|----------|-------------|--------|
| Device Name | [val] | [val] | [val] | [val] | ✓/✗ |
| EMDN Code | [val] | [val] | N/A | [val] | ✓/✗ |
| Intended Purpose | [val] | [val] | [val] | N/A | ✓/✗ |
| Literature Count | N/A | [val] | N/A | [val] | ✓/✗ |
| Benchmark Value X | N/A | [val] | [val] | N/A | ✓/✗ |

checklist: [ ] §2 specs = §4.2 equivalence table specs
[ ] §3 benchmark values = §4.7 analysis values
[ ] §4.4 literature count = Annex list count
[ ] §5 every claim has §4 cross-reference
[ ] Device name identical across ALL chapters
[ ] EMDN code identical across ALL chapters
[ ] MDR Class/Rule identical across ALL chapters
[ ] Version control log reflects all cross-chapter updates

common_pitfalls.pitfall: §2 specs updated but §4.2 table not updated
common_pitfalls.fix: Use cross-chapter validation matrix

common_pitfalls.pitfall: Literature count in text doesn't match Annex list
common_pitfalls.fix: Automated count verification script

common_pitfalls.pitfall: Device name typo in one section
common_pitfalls.fix: Find/replace verification before submission
Conclusion-to-analysis traceability audit

heuristics.injection_point: Gate layer — AFTER Writer Agent completes all sections, BEFORE submission packaging
heuristics.writer_guard_snippet: Cross-chapter consistency is NOT optional — it is a Blocker-level Gate check. Run the validation matrix on: device name, EMDN code, MDR Class/Rule, §2 specs vs §4.2 table, §3 benchmarks vs §4.7 values, §4.4 literature count vs Annex count, §5 claims vs §4 paragraphs. ANY mismatch = rewrite, not exception. DT-001 findings are 100% preventable with systematic validation.

injection_config.description: Heuristics are injected as a unified rule layer that applies across ALL Product Families and CER chapters
injection_config.injection_point: Between Slot Rules (Family-specific) and Section Defense (Chapter-specific)
injection_config.precedence: Slot Rules override Heuristics for family-specific conflicts; Heuristics override defaults
injection_config.activation: Always active for all CER generation tasks
